Or I don't know, it's just like, and there was even I even felt like peace. Like if you would be going, like I wouldn't even care. But oh, they have fun. Like, you could text me whenever you're done. Like, I don't want to be checking up on you.

definition: Responses that refer to the notion of "fighting kindly" or positive handling of conlict
example: [My partner] and I figured out how to fight kindly merely early in our marriage. Up until a couple of years ago, we would have a major blowout about every 6 to 12 months. But. I'm trying to think and I think we've had a blowout since I had cancer, and that was seven years ago. And so the good communication is, is that being able to openly just talk about this is what's going on. Are you okay with this? Is that going to happen? Right cause That alleviates a lot of the like the stories that we tell ourselves in terms of what actually is going to occur without actually confirming if that's going to occur or the story of this person is thinking this about me because they just acted that way versus just asking them what's going on? Are you pissed at me because you're seeming like you are? You know, so solid communication that way. And then in terms of when you're going to fail, it's the idea of fighting with the goal that nobody wins and you're fighting to try to move forward. In in solving something, in working together, in being able to move in a forward positive progression in the relationship. And it's okay if it doesn't get solved and settled. Not everything gets solved and settled. Like I. I don't like that you're doing that and you're still doing it. And so I will figure out what I'm going to do. But you keep doing that. And decide if I'm okay with sitting with that or not. Right. So. Yep.

definition: Responses that indicate growth or other positive outcomes post-conflict
example: I think there's also like a music and a relationship that happens, especially when you have different milestones, whether they're children, careers, sicknesses, etc.. And I think that all of the stressors that we've had. Have been ways for us to grow rather than to separate. And I think that's that's important in any relationship is that you'll always have something that comes up. That's not quite the script. And how you get through those moments and how you support each other in those moments and and how you also kind of inspect yourself in those moments. That's the important bit.

code: "never to go to bed mad" quick conflict resolution
definition: Responses that refer to quick conflict resolution
example: But, you know, we made a agreement a long time ago to never go to bed mad

definition: Responses that indicate behavioral adaptation towards each other, in the context of conflict resolution or prevention
example: We have to make some adjustments

definition: Responses that indicate the management of expectation/s within the relationship
example: And also like giving equal effort and equally meeting each other's needs and not setting, like, high expectations for your partner.

definition: Response that indicate relationship repair, especially after perceived damage or dysfunction. In contrast to the code 'conflict resolution' which broadly refer to ways of (and the importance of) resolving conflicts when they happen, this code refers to the notion of "coming back" once the perceived damage or dysfunction has been done
example: And so we really had to like, kind of come together and say, okay, look, this isn't working. We need to figure out how we work and reinvest in our relationship. And it was a reinvestment, an emotional and a physical and and just a friend way that that that helped to keep our marriage going

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to forgiveness as means of conflict resolution
example: The ability to forgive as big. Recognizing we all matter.

definition: Responses that explicity indicate "pettiness"
example: Pettiness, which I feel like also kind of ties into, like lack of communication. It's always like, oh, like he should have known that or, you know, maybe she shouldn't have done that. But they never, like, actually vocalize that stuff. So I feel like that's kind of like the heart of where bad relationships come from. Because, you know, there's like cheating and stuff like that. But it usually all comes down to like the rooted like something was missing in the relationship or something wasn't talked about. So I feel like pettiness was something that kind of a lot of people tend to do, and it leads to missing that part of a relationship

definition: Responses that explicity indicate "insecurity"
example: I guess constant fights, insecurities. Um... insecurities… lack of effort

definition: Responses that explicity indicate the perception of being forced to stay
example: Yeah. You know, I feel like. I feel like relationships that are kind of forced can sometimes be low quality. Kind of just stay together for the kids, but really, they don't really like each other that much. I feel like that can make a relationship like very low quality or. I don't know. So I'm marrying someone for money or something like that. Can make it low quality if, if you're gonna like low hanging fruit answers.

definition: Responses that explicity indicate perceived selfishness
example: Having a lot of trust issues. I'm always constantly worrying about what the other person is doing. Yeah. Being selfish, I think that's like reiterating because then, you know, you're not caring about what the other person is doing in a sense that benefits you, but you only care if it's not benefiting you. And then you like, you know, those treasured trust issues become anger, become jealousy or like envy. And then eventually you start to, like, lash out in different ways, like, you know, verbally, physically. Like, those are like, low quality relationship things.

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to maturity or immaturity often in the context of previous relationships
example: I think so much better. I mean, have grown up so much. I was in my previous relationship. I was immature, definitely. I've seen a lot of growth in me with my spouse's health. I've seen a lot of growth in her with my help. So we kind of like, you know, we we have an open conversation about things like that. So, like, what can we do to improve on each other? So yeah, I would definitely say it's I feel sorry for that my past relationship because that was like, terrible.

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to "seriousness" as it relates to relationships
example: The previous relationship was just like high school, like that young thing. I don't know. It's. Look, looking back on it, I mean, it's just like high school. Nothing. Yeah, nothing really serious or anything. Just a high school relationship.

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to being "toxic"

definition: Responses that refer to perceived (negative) degree of dependence among the couple
example: Being young definitely doesn't help, but that sort of thing, I it was just very codependent, as all and so was the second one. I kept that going. And that's why I ended up staying with him for so long was because of the huge factor being codependent with one another. I wouldn’t say  [my] relationship is very codependent. She has good relationships with some family members, so. That helps. And obviously it's hard to be codependent when you've got states between you.

definition: Responses that refer to infidelity
example: And that's probably that and we're gonna throw this in. You can't disagree about money. Because that's the thing that kills. That's probably that and infidelity are probably the two things that kill most relationships

definition: Responses that indicate to childrend or child-raising as potential stressors
example: But I get because young kids are terrible in a marriage

definition: Responses that indicate finances within the couple
example: I think stable sources of income are extremely important.

definition: Responses that refer to jealousy
example: I guess, like, jealousy. But on the, on the, like, extreme level.

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to being "controlling" within the relationship
example: To me, it's a. They're always together. Right. Someone's controlling. I don't think that's very good. They cheat on each other. I think that's a very big red flag, even though some open relationships work. So like I said, this is for me. Everyone has their own view on relationships.

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to withdrawal of interaction or isolation of oneself in the relationshiop
example: Would it be isolating yourself in anything that you're going through or anything like that? It's kind of hypocrite because I do that, but I need to try to work on that. Just like to not ball up your feelings and just dump to the side. You know, you have something to say. I say, speak your mind.

definition: Responses that refer to housework as a potential stressor in the relationship

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to being "healed" or recovering from previous issues
example: Yeah. I finally have peace within myself… Mm... And he helped also bring that out and like heal wounds that I didn't even know.

definition: Responses that refer to non-specific evaluations of the relationship or otherwise evaluative standards not captured by existing codes
example: Comparing my relationship with [my friend] I mean, I was better than some of my friends relationships, for sure, but I'd say like pretty on par with some of my other friends relationships.

definition: Responses that refer to the relationship of the individual to their family

definition: Responses that refer to the relationship of the individual to their partner's family
example: I think what makes our relationship high quality is that our families get along with each other. definition: Responses that explicitly refer to 'love languages'
example: Oh, and also, like, understanding each other's love languages.

definition: Responses that indicate of emotional contagion (i.e., of the emotion of an individual  affecting a similar emotion to the partner)

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to a feeling of completeness

definition: Responses that refer to the sense that the relationship is "natural" or have developed "organically"

definition: Responses that broadly refer to independence and agency within the relationship such as in pursuing activities and other goals without necessarily the involvement of the partner)
example: Send him off with his friends to go mountain climbing and you go off with your friends. Do whatever it is that he doesn't like to do, and then get back together and do something you do like to do together, I think you have to have things that you enjoy doing together

definition: Responses that explicitly refer to the notion of boundaries
example: So yeah. But. Yeah, I don't know. I think. I think my other friends are kind of having trouble either knowing how to look for compatibility between themselves and partners or knowing how to enforce their boundaries and ask for respect.

code: Acknowledgement that relationships are different for everybody
definition: Responses that acknowledge that relationships (and relationship quality) differ across individuals
example: To me, it's a. They're always together. Right. Someone's controlling. I don't think that's very good. They cheat on each other. I think that's a very big red flag, even though some open relationships work. So like I said, this is for me. Everyone has their own view on relationships.

definition: Responses that explicitly state that love and/or relationships are not perfect
example: The most important thing is. Number one, I think I would say that love is definitely not an easy thing, nor is it a perfect thing. You can't both both people have to accept that love is. Something where you kind of have to workout at the same time. It's not it's not going to be perfect. But if you're just true to it and you're kind of loyal to that love and that feeling and that affection, that will definitely lead to a happy, healthy relationship. And then also the same thing like you have to kind of it's not always 50/50. If one person can't do as much, then the other person should definitely be able to butt in and stand out like, Hey, you know, I can help you out. It's okay. Everything will be fine. Like that. Just those two are the key points.

definition: Responses that explicitly  refer to the notion of reciprocated effort among the couple
example: And I like a partner who's going to kind of reciprocate. What I give. And with the one that I'm in currently, I feel like I've given. You know all that I can. And he's done the same thing
